sections.about: This metric measures loans and advances given to related parties as a percentage of total loans and advances. It is a Section A 'Openness of Business' / related-party governance disclosure, linked to Principle 1 governance controls and conflict-of-interest management. A lower value indicates better performance because it suggests a smaller proportion of the company's lending activity is concentrated within the related-party ecosystem.
sections.performanceAndDrivers: In FY2022-23, SAIL had the lowest share at 1.81%, followed by Tata at 80%, JSW at 99.9%, and Jindal at 100%. In FY2023-24, SAIL remained the leader at 1.15%, Tata improved to 62%, JSW held at 99.94%, and Jindal remained at 100%. In FY2024-25, SAIL improved further to 0.8%, Tata continued improving to 43.62%, while JSW and Jindal both remained at 100%. SAIL's consistently low ratio reflects a PSU-style operating model where the listed entity's core plants, mines, and units are housed within the standalone boundary, reducing the need for intercompany advances. JSW and Jindal's persistent 100% concentration reflects group treasury structures where all advances flow to related entities. Tata's declining ratio across all three years indicates an ongoing reduction in related-party loan concentration. SAIL's BRSR also states that its related-party transaction policy governs transparency of approval and disclosures to manage conflicts of interest.
sections.tataPositioning: In FY2024-25, Tata ranks 2nd at 43.62%, behind SAIL at 0.8%. Tata has improved consistently across all three years: 80% in FY2022-23, 62% in FY2023-24, and 43.62% in FY2024-25, reflecting a directional reduction in related-party loan concentration. Despite the improvement, Tata remains well behind SAIL. The governance positioning for Tata should highlight controls — Board/Audit Committee oversight, arm's-length terms, business rationale for group funding, and clear RPT approval trails — rather than the ratio level alone.
sections.comparabilityNotes: FY2022-23 values for all four companies are prior-year comparatives reported in their respective FY2023-24 BRSRs. The denominator can vary sharply depending on whether a company has large third-party advances, project advances, employee loans, supplier advances, or intercompany loans in the reporting period.
